answer_text:
Quran 94:5-6 says: Indeed, there is ease with hardship. Most certainly, there is ease with hardship. Tafsir Ibn Kathir (English) emphasizes that these verses were revealed in Makkah and convey that just as Allah opens the breast and provides relief, with every hardship, there is also ease. 

Ma'arif al-Qur'an highlights that the repetition of ease indicates that after one hardship, multiple forms of ease follow, illustrating a divine promise of support during struggles.

Tafheem al-Qur'an explains that the phrase demonstrates reassurance to the Prophet (peace be upon him) that the challenges he faces are temporary and will be succeeded by better times. It reinforces the idea that ease closely accompanies hardship, almost as if they coexist.

tafsir_support.excerpt: Which was revealed in Makkah Ø¨ÙØ³ÙÙÙ Ø§ÙÙÙÙÙÙ Ø§ÙØ±ÙÙØ­ÙÙÙÙÙÙ Ø§ÙØ±ÙÙØ­ÙÙÙÙ In the Name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. The Meaning of opening the Breast Allah says, Ø£ÙÙÙÙÙ ÙÙØ´ÙØ±ÙØ­Ù ÙÙÙÙ ØµÙØ¯ÙØ±ÙÙÙ (Have We not opened your breast for you) meaning, `have We not opened your chest for you.' This means, `We illuminated it, and We made it spacious, vast and wide.' This is as Allah says, ÙÙÙÙÙ ÙÙØ±ÙØ¯Ù Ø§ÙÙÙÙÙÙ Ø£ÙÙ ÙÙÙÙØ¯ÙÙÙÙÙ ÙÙØ´ÙØ±ÙØ­Ù ØµÙØ¯ÙØ±ÙÙÙ ÙÙÙØ¥ÙØ³ÙÙÙÙÙÙ (And whomsoever Allah wills to guide, He opens his breast to Islam.) (6:125) And just as Allah expanded his chest, He also made His Law vast,
tafsir_support.text_html:
Which was revealed in Makkah

Ø¨ÙØ³ÙÙÙ Ø§ÙÙÙÙÙÙ Ø§ÙØ±ÙÙØ­ÙÙÙÙÙÙ Ø§ÙØ±ÙÙØ­ÙÙÙÙ

In the Name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful.

The Meaning of opening the Breast

Allah says,

Ø£ÙÙÙÙÙ ÙÙØ´ÙØ±ÙØ­Ù ÙÙÙÙ ØµÙØ¯ÙØ±ÙÙÙ

(Have We not opened your breast for you) meaning, `have We not opened your chest for you.' This means, `We illuminated it, and We made it spacious, vast and wide.' This is as Allah says,

ÙÙÙÙÙ ÙÙØ±ÙØ¯Ù Ø§ÙÙÙÙÙÙ Ø£ÙÙ ÙÙÙÙØ¯ÙÙÙÙÙ ÙÙØ´ÙØ±ÙØ­Ù ØµÙØ¯ÙØ±ÙÙÙ ÙÙÙØ¥ÙØ³ÙÙÙÙÙÙ

(And whomsoever Allah wills to guide, He opens his breast to Islam.) (6:125) And just as Allah expanded his chest, He also made His Law vast, wide, accommodating and easy, containing no difficulty, hardship or burden.

A Discussion of Allah's Favor upon His Messenger Concerning Allah's statement,

ÙÙÙÙØ¶ÙØ¹ÙÙÙØ§ Ø¹ÙÙÙÙ ÙÙØ²ÙØ±ÙÙÙ

(And removed from you your burden.) This means

ÙÙÙÙÙØºÙÙÙØ±Ù ÙÙÙÙ Ø§ÙÙÙÙÙÙ ÙÙØ§ ØªÙÙÙØ¯ÙÙÙÙ ÙÙÙ Ø°ÙÙØ¨ÙÙÙ ÙÙÙÙØ§ ØªÙØ£ÙØ®ÙÙØ±Ù

(That Allah may forgive you your sins of the past and the future.) (48:2)

Ø§ÙÙÙØ°ÙÙ Ø£ÙÙÙÙØ¶Ù Ø¸ÙÙÙØ±ÙÙÙ

(Which weighed down your back) Al-Inqad means the sound. And more than one of the Salaf has said concerning Allah's saying,

Ø§ÙÙÙØ°ÙÙ Ø£ÙÙÙÙØ¶Ù Ø¸ÙÙÙØ±ÙÙÙ

(Which weighed down your back) meaning, `its burden weighed heavy upon you.'

The Meaning of raising the Fame of the Prophet (Peace be upon him)

ÙÙØ±ÙÙÙØ¹Û¡ÙÙØ§ ÙÙÙÙ Ø°ÙÙÛ¡Ø±ÙÙÙ

(And have We not raised high your fame?) Mujahid said, "I (Allah) am not remembered except that you are remembered with Me: I bear witness that there is no God worthy of worship except Allah, and that Muhammad ï·º is the Messenger of Allah."

Qatadah said , "Allah raised his fame in this life and in the Hereafter. There is no one who gives a sermon, declares the Testimony of Faith (Shahadah), or prays a prayer (Salah) except that he proclaims it: I bear witness that there is no God worthy of worship except Allah, and that Muhammad ï·º is the Messenger of Allah.

Ease after Difficulty

Ø¥ÙÙÙÙ ÙÙØ¹Ù Ù±ÙÛ¡Ø¹ÙØ³Û¡Ø±Ù ÙÙØ³Û¡Ø±ÙÛ¬Ø§ â¢ ÙÙØ¥ÙÙÙÙ ÙÙØ¹Ù Ù±ÙÛ¡Ø¹ÙØ³Û¡Ø±Ù ÙÙØ³Û¡Ø±ÙØ§

(Verily along with every hardship is relief. Veriliy along with every hardship is relief)

Allah informs that with difficulty there is ease, and then He reaffirms this information (by repeating it)

The Command to remember Allah during Spare Time

ÙÙØ¥ÙØ°ÙØ§ ÙÙØ±ÙØºÛ¡ØªÙ ÙÙÙ±ÙØµÙØ¨Û¡ â¢ ÙÙØ¥ÙÙÙÙÙ° Ø±ÙØ¨ÙÙÙÙ ÙÙÙ±Ø±Û¡ØºÙØ¨

( So when you have finished, devote yourself to Allah's worship. And to your Lord turn intentions and hopes. )

Meaning, 'when you have completed your worldly affairs and its tasks, and you have broken away from its routine, then get up to perform the worship, and stand for it with zeal, complete devotion and purify your intention and desire for your lord.' Similar to this is the Prophet's statement in a hadith that is agreed-upon to be authentic,

ÙÙØ§ ØµÙÙÙØ§Ø©Ù Ø¨ÙØ­ÙØ¶ÙØ±ÙØ©Ù Ø§ÙØ·ÙÙØ¹ÙØ§ÙÙ, ÙÙÙÙØ§ ÙÙÙÙ ÙÙØ¯ÙØ§ÙÙØ¹ÙÙÙ Ø§ÙØ£ÙØ®ÙØ¨ÙØ«ÙØ§ÙÙ

There is no prayer when the food is served, nor when the two foul things (excrement and urine) are pressing a person.

The Prophet (ØµÙÙ Ø§ÙÙÙ Ø¹ÙÙÙ Ù Ø³ÙÙ) also said,

Ø¥ÙØ°ÙØ§ Ø£ÙÙÙÙÙÙØªÙ Ø§ÙØµÙÙÙÙØ§Ø©Ù ÙÙØ­ÙØ¶ÙØ±Ù Ø§ÙÙØ¹ÙØ´ÙØ§Ø¡Ù ÙÙØ¨ÙØ¯ÙØ£ÙÙØ§ Ø¨ÙØ§Ø§ÙÙÙØ¹ÙØ´ÙØ§Ø¡Ù

When the prayer has started and the dinner has been served, then begin with dinner.

Mujahid said concerning this Ayah, "When you are free from the worldly affairs, and you have stood to pray, then stand up for your Lord."

tafsir_support.excerpt: ÙÙØ¥ÙÙÙÙ ÙÙØ¹Ù Ø§ÙÙØ¹ÙØ³ÙØ±Ùâ ÙÙØ³ÙØ±ÙâØ§ Ø¥ÙÙÙÙ ÙÙØ¹Ù Ø§ÙÙØ¹ÙØ³ÙØ±Ùâ ÙÙØ³ÙØ±ÙâØ§ (So undoubtedly, along with hardship there is ease. Undoubtedly, along with hardship there is ease....94:6). Grammatically, if the Arabic definite article al- is prefixed to an Arabic noun and is repeated with the same definite article al-, they refer to the same antecedent. However, if the same noun is repeated without the definite article, they refer to different antecedents. The word al-'usr '[ the ] hardship' in verse [ 6] is the repetition of al-'usr '[ the ] hardship' occurring in verse [ 5].
tafsir_support.text_html:
ÙÙØ¥ÙÙÙÙ ÙÙØ¹Ù Ø§ÙÙØ¹ÙØ³ÙØ±Ùâ ÙÙØ³ÙØ±ÙâØ§ Ø¥ÙÙÙÙ ÙÙØ¹Ù Ø§ÙÙØ¹ÙØ³ÙØ±Ùâ ÙÙØ³ÙØ±ÙâØ§ (So undoubtedly, along with hardship there is ease. Undoubtedly, along with hardship there is ease....94:6). Grammatically, if the Arabic definite article al- is prefixed to an Arabic noun and is repeated with the same definite article al-, they refer to the same antecedent. However, if the same noun is repeated without the definite article, they refer to different antecedents. The word al-'usr '[ the ] hardship' in verse [ 6] is the repetition of al-'usr '[ the ] hardship' occurring in verse [ 5]. It does not refer to a new hardship. In contrast to this, the word yusr 'ease' in both verses occur without the definite article. This indicates that the second yusr 'ease' in verse [ 6] is a different antecedent to the yusr 'ease' occurring in verse [ 5]. Thus it may be concluded that there is only one ` usr 'hardship' and two yusr 'twofold ease'. 'Twofold ease' does not mean twice as much. In fact, it means 'manifold ease'. The verse signifies that only one kind of hardship will face him, but in the wake of it many kinds of ease are assured.

Sayyidna Hasan Al-Basri (Ø±Ø­) reports that once the Holy Prophet ï·º emerged from his home in a very happy mood and, giving cheerful news to his Companions on the basis of the current verse, said: "One hardship cannot overcome twofold ease". Thus history and biographical books written by Muslims and non-Muslims - all bear ample testimony to fact that the most difficult task, even the seemingly impossible task, became easy for him. The above narration further indicates that the Arabic definite article al- signifies that it is an article used to indicate previous knowledge [ that is, al- lil ` ahd ] and refers to the hardship of the Holy Prophet ï·º and his Companions. Allah kept to His promise to them in such a way that the world saw it visibly how in the wake of every hardship the Holy Prophet and his Companions experienced the manifold ease that made their task easy. If a person does not achieve 'ease' after 'hardship', it does not contradict this verse. In fact, even now Allah's universal principle applies. One needs to exercise fortitude against hardship, rely on Allah with purity of heart, devote oneself totally to Him, hold onto high hopes for His grace, and one should not despair of His mercy if there is delay in success - He certainly will grant relief after every instance of hardship. [ Fawa'id-e-` Usmaniah ]. Hadith narratives support this.

tafsir_support.excerpt: Indeed, there is ease with hardship.
tafsir_support.text_html: Indeed, there is ease with hardship.

tafsir_support.excerpt: On "Most certainly, there is ease with hardship.": This has been repeated twice so as to reassure the Prophet (peace be upon him) that the bad times he was passing through at that time would not last for ever, but were going to be replaced by good times in the near future. On the surface this appears to be a contradiction that hardship should be accompanied by ease, for these two things do not co-exist. But the words hardship with ease instead of ease after hardship have been used in the sense that the period of ease is so close to it as if it were a concomitant of it.
tafsir_support.text_html:
Verse wording: Most certainly, there is ease with hardship.

Commentary

  1. On âMost certainly, there is ease with hardship.â: This has been repeated twice so as to reassure the Prophet (peace be upon him) that the bad times he was passing through at that time would not last for ever, but were going to be replaced by good times in the near future. On the surface this appears to be a contradiction that hardship should be accompanied by ease, for these two things do not co-exist. But the words hardship with ease instead of ease after hardship have been used in the sense that the period of ease is so close to it as if it were a concomitant of it.

Ma'arif al-Qur'an highlights that the repetition of ease indicates that after one hardship, multiple forms of ease follow, illustrating a divine promise of support during struggles.

Tafheem al-Qur'an explains that the phrase demonstrates reassurance to the Prophet (peace be upon him) that the challenges he faces are temporary and will be succeeded by better times. It reinforces the idea that ease closely accompanies hardship, almost as if they coexist.

source_bundles.full_text:
ÙÙØ¥ÙÙÙÙ ÙÙØ¹Ù Ø§ÙÙØ¹ÙØ³ÙØ±Ùâ ÙÙØ³ÙØ±ÙâØ§ Ø¥ÙÙÙÙ ÙÙØ¹Ù Ø§ÙÙØ¹ÙØ³ÙØ±Ùâ ÙÙØ³ÙØ±ÙâØ§ (So undoubtedly, along with hardship there is ease. Undoubtedly, along with hardship there is ease....94:6). Grammatically, if the Arabic definite article al- is prefixed to an Arabic noun and is repeated with the same definite article al-, they refer to the same antecedent. However, if the same noun is repeated without the definite article, they refer to different antecedents. The word al-'usr '[ the ] hardship' in verse [ 6] is the repetition of al-'usr '[ the ] hardship' occurring in verse [ 5]. It does not refer to a new hardship. In contrast to this, the word yusr 'ease' in both verses occur without the definite article. This indicates that the second yusr 'ease' in verse [ 6] is a different antecedent to the yusr 'ease' occurring in verse [ 5]. Thus it may be concluded that there is only one ` usr 'hardship' and two yusr 'twofold ease'. 'Twofold ease' does not mean twice as much. In fact, it means 'manifold ease'. The verse signifies that only one kind of hardship will face him, but in the wake of it many kinds of ease are assured.

Sayyidna Hasan Al-Basri (Ø±Ø­) reports that once the Holy Prophet ï·º emerged from his home in a very happy mood and, giving cheerful news to his Companions on the basis of the current verse, said: "One hardship cannot overcome twofold ease". Thus history and biographical books written by Muslims and non-Muslims - all bear ample testimony to fact that the most difficult task, even the seemingly impossible task, became easy for him. The above narration further indicates that the Arabic definite article al- signifies that it is an article used to indicate previous knowledge [ that is, al- lil ` ahd ] and refers to the hardship of the Holy Prophet ï·º and his Companions. Allah kept to His promise to them in such a way that the world saw it visibly how in the wake of every hardship the Holy Prophet and his Companions experienced the manifold ease that made their task easy. If a person does not achieve 'ease' after 'hardship', it does not contradict this verse. In fact, even now Allah's universal principle applies. One needs to exercise fortitude against hardship, rely on Allah with purity of heart, devote oneself totally to Him, hold onto high hopes for His grace, and one should not despair of His mercy if there is delay in success - He certainly will grant relief after every instance of hardship. [ Fawa'id-e-` Usmaniah ]. Hadith narratives support this.

source_bundles.full_text:
Indeed, there is ease with hardship.

Verse wording:  Most certainly, there is ease with hardship.

Commentary    On âMost certainly, there is ease with hardship.â:  This has been repeated twice so as to reassure the Prophet (peace be upon him) that the bad times he was passing through at that time would not last for ever, but were going to be replaced by good times in the near future. On the surface this appears to be a contradiction that hardship should be accompanied by ease, for these two things do not co-exist. But the words hardship with ease instead of ease after hardship have been used in the sense that the period of ease is so close to it as if it were a concomitant of it.
source_bundles.focused_extract: Indeed, there is ease with hardship.
source_bundles.short_excerpt: Indeed, there is ease with hardship.

units.excerpt: Indeed, there is ease with hardship.

On "Most certainly, there is ease with hardship.": This has been repeated twice so as to reassure the Prophet (peace be upon him) that the bad times he was passing through at that time would not last for ever, but were going to be replaced by good times in the near future. On the surface this appears to be a contradiction that hardship should be accompanied by ease, for these two things do not co-exist. But the words hardship with ease instead of ease after hardship have been used in the sense that the period of ease is so close to it as if it were a concomitant of it.
